role_summary: Sasser, Georgia native; participant in the early-1960s Southwest Georgia Movement organized by Rev. Charles Sherrod (catalog entry 18) and the Albany-Movement-affiliated SNCC field network in Worth, Terrell, and Sumter Counties.
ai_reading: Browner's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 78), with Johnnie Ruth McCullar (entry 65) as the top match and the broader cluster including Juadine Henderson (entry 68), Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121), and Charles McLaurin (entry 17). The novel observation his page surfaces is the Southwest-Georgia-cohort signature: Browner, Robert McClary (catalog entry 109), and the joint Charles Sherrod (entry 18) and William G. Anderson (entry 126) interviews document the same Sherrod-led SNCC organizing tradition that anchored the Albany Movement; the catalog's clustering of these Southwest Georgia voices suggests the embedding reads the Worth-Terrell-Sumter County rural-organizing register as a coherent vector signature distinct from the more-photographed Mississippi-and-Alabama field-organizer cohorts.
biographical_paragraph: Clifford D. Browner Jr.'s Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. The external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 23 [src: 1]. Browner grew up in Sasser, Georgia (Terrell County, in the Southwest Georgia rural-cotton belt) and came of age in the early 1960s when the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project, led by Rev. Charles Sherrod (catalog entry 18) and based in Albany, was organizing voter registration and mass meetings across the surrounding counties [src: 1]. His oral history conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany in March 2013 documents how he came to be involved in the Southwest Georgia Movement and his organizing work alongside the SNCC field staff of that project.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Browner describes the mass meetings held at Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Sasser after the Southwest Georgia Project moved them there:
interview_snippets.quote: They were excited meetings. I mean, you know, people, the first time I really saw blacks and whites intermingling in a close vicinity that way was at those meetings.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the action he and a classmate took at George Washington Carver High School, where Black students received textbooks passed down from the white school:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, the mass meeting really wasn't focused in a lot of ways, but at school, Clovis Jones and I wrote a letter to the superintendent. And I can't remember his name at this time, but we wrote a letter to him asking for new books because the one we were getting were hamming down from the white school.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: He recalls the morning after night riders burned Mt. Olive Baptist Church to the ground, when he and his father went to the site:
interview_snippets.quote: But I can remember sitting on the ground and you can see if anybody would burn a church there, do anything. And that was one of the statements I remember saying to myself, as I stood in the watch, the smoke.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why he enlisted in the Army in July 1964 after graduating from high school:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, one thing my father could not afford to send me to college. He and I had the conversation one morning said, you know, baby, I just can't afford to pay for college. I said, don't worry about that, I take care of it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting at the close of the interview on the difference he found between the South and the North after moving to Flint, Michigan:
interview_snippets.quote: But that's the difference I see between North and South. There's a perceived freedom. But it really is not there sometimes.

movement_context: Terrell County, where Browner grew up, was identified in the early-1960s SNCC field strategy as one of the four "hardcore" Southwest Georgia counties (alongside Lee, Worth, and Baker) the Sherrod-led Project prioritized for voter registration; the August 8, 1962 Mt. Olive Baptist Church arson in Sasser (one of three rural Black churches burned in Terrell County across August and September 1962 in retaliation for the SNCC voter-registration work) was the local-violence event that brought James Forman and U.S. Department of Justice Civil Rights Division attorneys to the county in the late summer of 1962 [src: 2]. The September 9, 1962 federal-court filing United States v. Raines, et al. in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Georgia was the Department of Justice's response, an injunction action against Terrell County Sheriff Z. T. "Zeke" Mathews and his deputies, that produced the first federal-court order specifically protecting voter-registration workers under the 1957 and 1960 Civil Rights Acts [src: 2]. Browner's organizing work belongs to that 1962-1965 Terrell County project's local-volunteer base, the cohort the Hasan Kwame Jeffries oral-history series for the Civil Rights History Project documented across his 2013 fieldwork in Albany and the surrounding counties [src: 1, 3].

gallery.alt: Terrell County Courthouse in Dawson, Georgia
gallery.caption: The Terrell County Courthouse in Dawson, Georgia, the county seat. The 1962 voter-registration actions Browner's Sasser-and-Terrell-County organizing belongs to ran through this courthouse; the September 1962 United States v. Raines injunction action against Sheriff Z. T. Mathews and his deputies named this courthouse and its officers as defendants.
